summary:
One of the most distinctive and widespread ritual practices of the [[event:18|Pre-Pottery Neolithic B]] Levant was the deliberate removal of human skulls after death, their subsequent treatment with white lime plaster to reconstruct lifelike features — including painted eyes of shell or bitumen, modeled noses and lips, and painted skin tones — and their curation and display as objects of veneration. This practice, attested at sites including [[event:2|Jericho]], [[event:7|Ain Ghazal]], Tell Ramad, Kfar Hahoresh, Basta, and other PPNB communities, represents some of the most vivid evidence for ancestor veneration and ritual treatment of the dead in human prehistory.

The plastered skulls were not hidden away but appear to have been displayed, possibly in domestic shrines or communal spaces, before eventually being deposited in pits in groups. Analysis of the plaster-covered skulls reveals great artisanal skill and individualized treatment — each skull was rendered with distinct facial features, suggesting that the identity and continued spiritual presence of specific ancestors was being maintained and honored. The practice appears to have been widespread enough to constitute a shared cultural tradition across a broad geographic area during the PPNB period.

The skull cult practices of the Levantine Neolithic are interpreted by most archaeologists as evidence of ancestor worship — a belief that the spirits or powers of deceased community members could be accessed or appeased through the preservation and veneration of their physical remains. This form of ritual behavior reflects the development of complex spiritual and social practices accompanying the transition to settled agricultural life, including new relationships to land, property, lineage, and identity that sedentism and inheritance created.
